metadata.title: Why a Probability Density Can Be Greater Than One
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = probability density / correct interpretation / integration as area; CRIMSON = student confusion / wrong direct-value reading / misinterpreted as probability
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no normalization calculation, no Born-rule derivation, no momentum-space counterpart. Wonder register.

beats.narration_text: med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: A quantum textbook shows the vertical bar psi-squared equals 2 at a point. A student flags it as impossible — probabilities can't exceed one. The student is wrong.

card.copy: Why a Probability Density Can Be Greater Than One

beats.narration_text: The Born rule says the vertical bar psi of x squared gives the probability. Here that quantity equals 2 at a point. That's greater than one. It looks like a contradiction. Why isn't it?

production_viz.label: density curve with peak > 1 on y-axis; student confusion arrow

beats.narration_text: The probability of finding the particle at exactly one point is zero — a point has zero width. You need an interval. The vertical bar psi of x squared is not a probability; it's a probability density. Density times width gives probability.

card.copy: |ψ(x)|² is probability per unit length — not probability. You must integrate over an interval to get a number between 0 and 1.

beats.narration_text: Units: the vertical bar psi of x squared has units of per meter — or per nanometer. To get a dimensionless probability you integrate over an interval: P of a to b equals the integral from a to b of the vertical bar psi squared d-x. The units cancel: per-nanometer times nanometer equals dimensionless.

production_viz.label: density curve, strip area = probability, units: nm^-1 * nm = 1

beats.narration_text: Example: psi of x equals 1 over root-a times e to the minus absolute-x over a, with a equals 0.5 nanometers. Peak density: 1 over a equals 2 per nanometer. Probability within plus or minus 0.1 nanometers: integrate 2 times e to the minus 2 absolute-x over 0.5 — result about 0.33. Sensible: between 0 and 1.

A density of 2 per nanometer over a 0.1 nanometer interval gives a probability of about 0.2. Over a 0.5 nanometer interval: about 0.63. Over a very large interval: the total approaches 1. The density can be large wherever the particle is concentrated. The integral over all space is always exactly 1.

production_viz.label: different interval widths: probability changes, total = 1

beats.narration_text: Think of population density. New York City: about 27,000 people per square kilometer. That number is not a probability. The probability that a random resident lives in one specific square kilometer is 27,000 divided by 8 million — about 0.3 percent. High density, perfectly valid probability.

production_viz.label: city density 27000/km2 >> 1; probability for one block << 1

beats.narration_text: The harmonic oscillator ground state has a Gaussian density with peak value square-root of m-omega over pi-h-bar. For a typical quantum dot, this peak can be 10 per nanometer. Not a contradiction. The normalization integral — the total probability over all space — is still exactly 1.

production_viz.label: harmonic oscillator peak density >> 1; integral over all x = 1

beats.narration_text: Probability density is what quantum mechanics directly delivers. To get the probability for any measurement, integrate over the measurement window. The density can be large wherever the particle is likely to be found — as long as the total integral over all space is exactly 1.

production_viz.label: P(interval) = integral of |psi|^2 dx; total = 1 always

beats.narration_text: The vertical bar psi of x squared is per-meter, not dimensionless. Probability equals integral of psi-squared d-x over an interval. Density can exceed 1. Total probability equals 1. No contradiction.

card.copy: |ψ(x)|² is per-meter. Probability = ∫|ψ|²dx over an interval. Density > 1 allowed. Total = 1.

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
